50.note: He has grasped the horns of the altar saying, Let King Solomon swear to me first that he will not put his servant to death by the sword — the altar as a refuge for one who fears death echoes the Mosaic provision that the unintentional killer could flee to a city of refuge, but that someone who kills presumptuously must be dragged from the altar to die (Exod 21:14). Adonijah's flight to the altar is an instinctive appeal to sacred asylum; his survival depends on Solomon's judgment of his motives, not on any guaranteed right.

2.note: Be strong and show yourself a man. Keep the charge of the LORD your God, walk in his ways — David's deathbed charge to Solomon repeats nearly verbatim the Deuteronomic leadership-transition formula that YHWH gave to Joshua at Moses's death. The pattern (courage command + Torah-obedience command + consequence promise) marks every major leadership succession in Israel's narrative: Moses to Joshua, YHWH to Joshua, David to Solomon.

10.note: David slept with his fathers and was buried in the city of David — Peter's Pentecost sermon builds its resurrection argument precisely on this datum: David died and was buried, and his tomb is with us to this day (Acts 2:29). The phrase from 1 Kgs 2:10 becomes the theological foil for Peter's Christological claim: unlike David, whose body remained in the tomb, the one about whom David prophesied was raised without seeing corruption. The formulaic death-and-burial notice for David makes the resurrection of Jesus all the more significant.

Once for all I have sworn by my holiness — I will not lie to David. His offspring shall endure forever, his throne as long as the sun before me — the tension between Solomon sitting firmly on David's throne and the conditional formulation of 2:4 (if your sons keep my ways) is the theological problem that Ps 89 explicitly explores. The psalmist holds the unconditional oath (v35-37) against the apparent failure of the dynasty (v38-45) and cries out for its fulfillment. The NT resolution: Christ inherits the unconditional oath and keeps it perfectly.

27.note: So Solomon expelled Abiathar from being priest to the LORD, thus fulfilling the word of the LORD that he had spoken concerning the house of Eli at Shiloh — the narrator explicitly names this as prophetic fulfillment of the man of God's oracle to Eli (1 Sam 2:31-36: I will cut off your arm and the arm of your father's house). The removal of Abiathar closes a prophetic arc that opened in Samuel; the narrator's formula shows awareness of scripture's own coherence across generations.

9.note: If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God, who gives generously to all without reproach — James's invitation to ask God for wisdom is built on the paradigm case of Solomon's request in 1 Kgs 3:9. Solomon is the model: he asked not for riches or long life but for a discerning heart, and received not only the wisdom but the other gifts besides. James's logic assumes readers know the Solomon-story: this is how wisdom-asking works.

In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge — the Solomonic wisdom that surpassed all (v12: there will never have been anyone like you) is treated by Paul as a type that Christ fulfills and surpasses. In Solomon, divine wisdom was granted to a man; in Christ, it is constitutively present in a person. The wisdom Solomon sought from outside himself is what Jesus is (1 Cor 1:30: Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God).

20.note: Judah and Israel were as many as the sand by the sea — the sand-of-the-sea image for Israel's population in 1 Kgs 4:20 deliberately recalls YHWH's oath to Abraham (Gen 22:17: I will multiply your offspring as the sand that is on the seashore). The narrator signals that the Solomonic golden age is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise: prosperity from the Euphrates to the border of Egypt, innumerable people, peace on every side.

5.note: I intend to build a house for the name of the LORD my God, as the LORD said to David my father... You shall not build the house for my name, because you are a man of war — 1 Chr 22:9-10 records YHWH's word to David more fully: a son born to you shall be a man of rest... he shall be Solomon, and I will give peace and quiet to Israel in his days; he shall build a house for my name. Solomon's temple-building fulfills a specific oracle concerning the man of peace. The David/Solomon contrast (warrior vs. peace-giver, warrior who sheds blood vs. the one who builds the sanctuary) is a figure of the two-age structure that finds its antitype in Christ — the one in whom the true temple is built.

1.note: Solomon builds the magnificent temple at Jerusalem — the culmination of Israel's worship-system. Stephen's sermon turns the temple against itself: 'Yet the Most High does not dwell in houses made by hands' (Acts 7:48), citing Isaiah 66:1-2. The temple's construction is real and divinely commissioned; yet its very glory highlights that God cannot be domesticated by any human structure, however magnificent.

12.note: God promises to dwell in the temple if Solomon keeps his statutes: 'I will dwell among the children of Israel and will not forsake my people Israel.' Paul cites the covenant-dwelling formula in 2 Corinthians 6:16: 'we are the temple of the living God; as God said, I will make my dwelling among them and walk among them.' The conditional Solomonic promise becomes the unconditional new covenant fulfillment in the Spirit-indwelt community.

God's conditional promise to dwell in the Jerusalem temple — dependent on covenant obedience — is now unconditionally realized in the believer's body: 'your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you' (1 Cor 6:19). What Solomon built of cedar and stone, the Spirit inhabits in human flesh without waiting for human perfection.

20.note: The Most Holy Place is a perfect cube — twenty cubits in length, width, and height. This is the only room in Scripture described as a perfect cube. John's New Jerusalem is also described as a perfect cube: 'its length and width and height are equal' (Rev 21:16). The shape of the Most Holy Place — the place of God's presence, accessible only to the high priest once a year — becomes the shape of the city where God dwells with his people permanently and all have access.

23.note: The two massive golden cherubim of olive wood stand in the Most Holy Place, their wings spanning ten cubits each. Hebrews 9:5 notes them as the 'cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat' — guardians of the divine presence. The same creatures appear in Ezekiel's and John's visions (Ezek 1; Rev 4:6-8), transformed into the four living creatures who unceasingly worship before the throne they once guarded.

38.note: Solomon's temple, seven years in building (v.38), is the high point of OT worship. Jesus identifies himself as the true temple: when challenged to show a sign, he says 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.' John explains: 'he was speaking about the temple of his body' (2:21). What took seven years to build and centuries to embody now stands in the person of the eternal Son — the dwelling of God in human flesh.

15.note: The two bronze pillars Jachin ('He will establish') and Boaz ('In him is strength') stand at the entrance to the temple — named, enduring, marking the threshold of the divine dwelling. Christ's promise to the overcomer in Revelation 3:12 takes up the pillar image: 'I will make him a pillar in the temple of my God. Never shall he go out of it.' The physical pillars that marked the earthly temple are transformed into the permanent standing of the redeemed in the eternal temple.

21.note: The pillars Jachin and Boaz are named — they are structural, identity-bearing supports of the temple. Paul describes the church as 'built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone, in whom the whole structure...grows into a holy temple in the Lord' (Eph 2:20-21). The named pillars of the Solomonic temple are the OT image of what the apostolic foundation becomes in the new covenant architecture.

23.note: The bronze sea — a massive round basin ten cubits in diameter — is the temple's washing vessel, used for priestly purification before service. The OT priestly washing (required for access to the holy things) is the type of baptism: Peter declares 'baptism...now saves you, not as a removal of dirt from the body but as an appeal to God for a good conscience, through the resurrection of Jesus Christ' (1 Pet 3:21). The bronze sea's purification-for-service becomes baptism's purification-for-ministry.

The sea held water for the priests' mandatory washing before serving in the sanctuary — without it, service was impossible. Paul's nuptial image uses the same purification language for Christ and the church: 'that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word' (Eph 5:26). The priestly washing-basin's purpose (purity for service before a holy God) is applied to the whole new covenant community.

48.note: The accumulated furnishings of Solomon's temple — the golden altar, the table for the bread of the Presence, the lampstands, the utensils — are all part of a system that mediates between the holy God and an impure people. Revelation 21:22 announces the eschatological resolution: 'I saw no temple in the city, for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb.' In the new creation, the mediation-system is dissolved because God himself is the temple — direct access is permanent.

10.note: The cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle, so that Moses was not able to enter — the cloud filling the newly completed temple at its dedication (v10-11) exactly recapitulates the cloud-filling of the tabernacle at Sinai. YHWH's acceptance of the new sacred structure is expressed by the same sign as before: his glory descends and displaces the human ministers. The temple-dedication theophany authenticates the temple as the tabernacle's successor.

27.note: But will God indeed dwell on the earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you, how much less this house that I have built — Solomon's prayer at the temple dedication acknowledges divine transcendence; Stephen quotes Isa 66:1-2 to make the same point: the Most High does not dwell in temples made by human hands; both point toward the incarnation as the true dwelling of God among his people (John 1:14)

Solomon built him a house. Yet the Most High does not dwell in houses made by human hands — Stephen's speech cites Isa 66:1-2 to argue that the temple does not contain God, but what is remarkable is that Solomon himself made this same argument in his own dedication prayer. The prayer that inaugurates the temple undercuts any theology of divine domestication: heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you; how much less this house. The temple's own builder was a theologian of YHWH's transcendence.

41.note: The foreigners who join themselves to the LORD... their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be accepted on my altar; for my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples — Isaiah's expansion of the foreigner's welcome develops Solomon's foreigner-prayer (v41-43: all peoples may know your name). Jesus quotes Isa 56:7 when cleansing the temple (Mark 11:17), invoking a vision that Solomon himself had prayed for in the same location. The temple-cleansing was a protest that the temple had failed to be what even its builder had asked YHWH to make it: a house open to all nations.

7.note: Then I will cut off Israel from the land... and this house I will cast out of my sight — Jesus's prediction of the temple's total destruction (not one stone will be left on another, Matt 24:2) is the fulfillment of YHWH's own conditional warning to Solomon. The very dedication ceremony that enshrined the temple as the eternal home of YHWH's name also contained the threat that disobedience would lead to its total repudiation. The disciples marveling at the temple buildings (Matt 24:1) are standing in the shadow of a conditionality Solomon was warned about at the start.

1.note: The Queen of Sheba travels 'from the ends of the earth' to hear Solomon's wisdom. Jesus invokes her against his generation: 'the queen of the South will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here' (Matt 12:42). A Gentile queen who sought merely human wisdom becomes the foil for those who have the incarnate Word and remain indifferent.

Luke's parallel: 'the queen of the South will rise up at the judgment with the men of this generation and condemn them, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.' The queen's costly pursuit of wisdom is a judgment-standard: what she sought across continents is now present in person.

23.note: Solomon exceeded all kings in wealth — his glory was the summit of human achievement. Jesus invokes Solomonic splendor only to supersede it: 'even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these' (wildflowers of the field, Matt 6:29). The Father's care for creation surpasses the most glorious kingdom; therefore anxiety about provision is faithlessness, not prudence.

1.note: Solomon's 700 wives and 300 concubines from foreign nations 'turned his heart after other gods' (v.4). Paul's command 'do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers' (2 Cor 6:14) has Solomon's apostasy as its ultimate cautionary example: the wisest man who ever lived was brought down by covenant-violating intimate relationships that drew his devotion toward other gods.

36.note: God promises to preserve 'one tribe to my servant David, that David my servant may always have a lamp before me in Jerusalem.' The preserved Davidic lamp — kept burning through political catastrophe, exile, and return — is the type of the messianic dawn Zechariah celebrates: 'the sunrise shall visit us from on high' (Luke 1:78). The lamp YHWH maintained for David's sake in Jerusalem finds its final flame in the one who is the light of the world.

The Davidic lamp (v.36) — YHWH's commitment to sustain the line — points toward Revelation 21:23: 'the city has no need of sun or moon...for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb.' The lamp YHWH promised David is ultimately the Lamb himself — the light the darkness has never extinguished (John 1:5).

1.note: Elijah was a man with a nature like ours, and he prayed fervently that it might not rain, and for three years and six months it did not rain on the earth — James cites Elijah's prayer as an example of effective righteous prayer; the three-and-a-half-year drought is applied typologically in Revelation (11:6) to the witnesses' power

8.note: In truth I tell you, there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah ... and Elijah was sent to none of them but only to Zarephath, a city of Sidon, to a woman who was a widow — Jesus cites the widow of Zarephath (1 Kgs 17:8-16) as an OT precedent for divine grace going to a Gentile; the Nazareth congregation's rejection of Jesus follows their rejection of this theological point

10.note: I have been very jealous for YHWH the God of hosts. For the people of Israel have forsaken your covenant, thrown down your altars, and killed your prophets with the sword, and I, even I only, am left — Paul cites Elijah's complaint and YHWH's response (seven thousand who have not bowed to Baal) as proof that God has not rejected Israel; the remnant principle is the OT basis for Israel's partial hardening and the elect remnant within
Elijah's complaint — I am the only one left

18.note: Seven thousand reserved who have not bowed to Baal
